title: chore: remove nofeed and private image params
author: ElliotEtag
pr_body_excerpt:
Follow-up cleanup to #11712. Removes two more inert image params.

- **`nofeed`** — there is no image feed in the current gen worker. The parsed value is never read to gate publishing (no feed-publish code, no `/feed` route, not in the tracking path); it was only force-set on the v1 routes and stripped from the cache key.
- **`private`** (image) — just an alias folded into `nofeed` in `ImageParamsSchema` (`nofeed = data.nofeed || data.private`), so it did nothing on its own.

Removed from: `ImageParamsSchema`, the v1 image/edit routes (`nofeed: true` + `private` passthrough), the SDK image/video options (types + client + README), the MCP image tool schemas/forwarders, and the example/social scripts.

Deliberately left untouched:
- The **text** `private`/`isPrivate` flag (`text/requestUtils.ts` etc.) — a separate, live feature.
- `media-cache.ts` `EXCLUDED_PARAMS` still lists `nofeed`, so legacy/community clients still sending `?nofeed=true` keep sharing the cache (avoids fragmentation). Behavior-neutral.

No public OpenAPI/APIDOCS change — these were never declared in the GET image query schema.

Verified: gen + SDK typecheck, biome, gen image + index tests (57) pass.

Note: overlaps with #11712 (same files); whichever merges second will need a trivial rebase.

gist.headline: Sweeping up the hive
gist.blurb: We've done some spring cleaning in the codebase, removing the old nofeed and private image parameters that weren't doing anything anymore. Just keeping the hive tidy!
gist.summary: Removed the inert nofeed and private image parameters from the generation worker, SDK, and MCP tools.
gist.impact: Developers will see cleaner SDK types and MCP schemas, while legacy requests using these parameters will continue to work without cache fragmentation.
gist.visual_concept: Polly the bee sweeping up old code snippets or dead leaves in a cozy digital garden. A chunky robot monitor holds a dustpan.

A chunky robot with a CRT monitor head holds a dustpan, screen glowing lime green (#ecf874). Soft pastel lavender and cream gradient background. Warm lighting, lo-fi 8-bit aesthetic like Stardew Valley. Nostalgic but beautiful, emotionally warm.
